description: Go runtime health dashboard covering CPU, memory, garbage collection, scheduling, contention, and file descriptor pressure. The dashboards were generated using [go-mixin](https://github.com/adinhodovic/go-mixin). Open issues and create feature requests in the repository.

panels.description: Number of targets currently exposing Go runtime metrics for the selected filters. Use it to confirm scope before comparing totals or averages.
panels.title: Instances

panels.description: Average resident memory across the selected targets. Start here for memory-pressure checks: it reflects memory currently resident in RAM and is the fastest way to spot leaks, step changes in working set size, or steady growth that needs a deeper look in Runtime Footprint and Heap Breakdown.
panels.title: Memory Used

panels.description: Total CPU cores consumed by the selected Go processes. Start here for saturation checks: if this climbs unexpectedly, compare it with scheduler latency and then use the per-target CPU panel to see whether one target or the whole fleet is driving the increase.
panels.title: CPU Cores

panels.description: Current goroutine count across all selected targets. Watch for sharp growth, sustained elevation, or leaks.
panels.title: Goroutines

panels.description: Runtime settings reported by each target, including Go version, GOMAXPROCS, and GOMEMLIMIT when it is set.
panels.title: Pod Runtime Config

panels.description: CPU cores consumed by each pod, container, and instance. Use this after the aggregate CPU stat to see whether elevated usage is concentrated in a few hot targets or spread across the fleet, which is often the quickest clue about whether you are dealing with skew, noisy neighbors, or broad workload growth.
panels.title: CPU Usage by Pod / Container / Instance

panels.description: Goroutine count by pod, container, and instance. Use it to find which targets are carrying the most concurrency.
panels.title: Goroutines by Pod / Container / Instance

panels.description: Runnable goroutines waiting to be scheduled by pod, container, and instance. Growth here points to scheduler backlog on specific targets.
panels.title: Runnable Goroutines by Pod / Container / Instance

panels.description: Operating system threads used by each Go process, grouped by pod, container, and instance. Use it to spot thread growth from blocking work or cgo.
panels.title: Threads by Pod / Container / Instance

panels.description: How long runnable goroutines wait to be scheduled. This is the main contention signal in the dashboard: rising P95 or P99 usually means scheduler pressure, CPU saturation, or overloaded targets, especially when runnable goroutines and thread counts are increasing at the same time.
panels.title: Scheduler Latency

panels.description: Share of CPU time spent in garbage collection over time. Start here for GC-pressure checks: it separates brief spikes from sustained GC cost and helps answer whether elevated CPU is coming from application work or from the runtime spending more time reclaiming memory.
panels.title: GC CPU Ratio

panels.description: Resident memory by pod, container, and instance. This is the clearest per-target view of process memory use.
panels.title: Memory Usage by Pod / Container / Instance

panels.description: Runtime memory footprint compared with the next GC target and configured memory limit. This is the key interpretation panel for memory pressure: it shows whether current growth still fits within the runtime's expected GC envelope or whether the process is pushing toward its next GC goal or a configured cap such as GOMEMLIMIT.
panels.title: Runtime Footprint

panels.description: Heap and runtime memory classes across the selected targets. Use it to see where memory is held, not just the total amount.
panels.title: Heap Breakdown

panels.description: Bytes the garbage collector must scan across heap, globals, and stacks. This is a strong proxy for overall GC scan pressure.
panels.title: GC Scan Pressure

panels.description: Heap target for the next GC cycle in total and by pod, container, and instance. Rising targets show the runtime allowing more live heap before collecting.
panels.title: Next GC Target

panels.description: Allocation size percentiles. Larger allocations can change GC behavior even when allocation counts stay flat.
panels.title: Heap Allocation Size

panels.description: CPU time spent in each GC and scavenger phase when the runtime exposes those metrics. Missing series are normal in some environments.
panels.title: GC CPU Phase

panels.description: Garbage collection cycle frequency, split between automatic and forced runs. Use it to see whether pressure comes from steady churn or forced work.
panels.title: GC Cycles

panels.description: GC duration quantiles reported by the Go runtime. This complements pause histograms with the runtime's own summary view.
panels.title: GC Duration by Quantile

panels.description: Stop-the-world pause latency percentiles. This is usually the most user-visible GC panel for request-driven services: sustained increases here often correlate with tail-latency regressions even when total GC CPU looks acceptable, so it is a good place to confirm whether GC is affecting service behavior.
panels.title: GC Pause Latency

panels.description: Object allocation and free rate. Large gaps between allocs and frees can point to live-heap growth or churn-heavy workloads.
panels.title: Heap Object Churn

panels.description: Byte allocation and free throughput. Use it to measure how much heap traffic the runtime is processing over time.
panels.title: Heap Byte Churn

panels.description: Open file descriptors by pod, container, and instance. Pair it with FD ratio to compare absolute usage with limit pressure.
panels.title: Open File Descriptors by Pod / Container / Instance

panels.description: Open file descriptors divided by the configured maximum, grouped by pod, container, and instance. Start here for exhaustion checks: it normalizes raw descriptor usage against each target's limit, makes unlike targets comparable, and shows who is actually closest to running out.
panels.title: File Descriptor Usage Ratio by Pod / Container / Instance

panels.description: Time spent waiting on mutexes by pod, container, and instance. Rising values can indicate lock contention or blocked coordination.
panels.title: Mutex Wait by Pod / Container / Instance

panels.description: Rate of cgo callbacks into Go by pod, container, and instance. Use it to explain thread growth, scheduler shifts, or CPU patterns tied to native code.
panels.title: cgo Calls Rate by Pod / Container / Instance

title: Commute Go Internals
